title: Pasta Carbonara (Egg and Mushroom Adaptation)

subs.name: thick rice noodles or wheat noodles
subs.notes: cook separately and drain well; the sauce works on any noodle

subs.name: 150 ml silken tofu blended smooth for a vegan version — the texture is different but creamy
subs.notes: omit dairy cheese too; season generously with nutritional yeast

ingredients.name: Pecorino Romano or Parmesan, finely grated

subs.name: 50 g processed cheese block, finely grated
subs.notes: less sharp than Pecorino but melts smoothly into the sauce

ingredients.name: button mushrooms, sliced thinly
visual_checks: replaces guanciale; adds umami and texture
ingredients.notes: replaces guanciale; adds umami and texture

subs.name: 200 g smoked tofu, diced and pan-fried until crispy
subs.notes: adds smokiness; use soy sauce to season for extra depth

steps.text: In a bowl, whisk together the eggs, yolks and grated cheese with the cracked black pepper until completely smooth. Set aside at room temperature — cold eggs will seize on contact with the hot pasta.
Cook the pasta in a large pot of well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve at least 250 ml of pasta water before draining.

While pasta cooks, heat olive oil in a large frying pan over high heat. Add mushrooms in one layer and sear without stirring for 2–3 minutes until golden and caramelised. Add the garlic cloves (whole) and toss for 1 more minute, then discard garlic.
Drain the pasta and immediately add to the mushroom pan. Remove the pan completely from heat — this is critical. Let the pasta cool for 30 seconds so the pan is no longer scorching.

Pour the egg-cheese mixture over the pasta and toss continuously, adding pasta water a few tablespoons at a time. The residual heat will gently cook the eggs into a glossy, flowing sauce. If it looks dry, add more pasta water; if it looks like it's scrambling, add cold water immediately and toss fast. Aim for a sauce that coats each strand thickly.
Plate immediately into warm bowls. Top with extra grated cheese, a generous twist of black pepper and serve at once — carbonara does not wait.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: mushroom → 200 g smoked tofu, diced and pan-fried until crispy (adds smokiness; use soy sauce to season for extra depth); cheese → 50 g processed cheese block, finely grated (less sharp than Pecorino but melts smoothly into the sauce); egg → 150 ml silken tofu blended smooth for a vegan version — the texture is different but creamy (omit dairy cheese too; season generously with nutritional yeast); pasta → thick rice noodles or wheat noodles (cook separately and drain well; the sauce works on any noodle)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use a kadai or any wide thick-bottomed pan. Amul Parmesan (in the refrigerator aisle) or any block cheese finely grated works well. The key challenge is temperature — once you drain the pasta and add the egg mixture, work very fast and keep the pan off the burner. Mushrooms are available at most vegetable markets in metros; button mushrooms from the supermarket are ideal. This dish works without any typically Italian ingredient being hard to find.
